coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as potential antioxidants for the scavenging of multi-reactive oxygen and nitrogen species
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Fucheng
Liu, Jiamei
Gong, Pengyu
Yang, Yufei
Jiang, Yanyan
coredata.dc:description: Selenium (Se) and metal-doped carbon dots (CDs) based nanoenzymes have bright prospects as antioxidants; however, the high cost of Se and potential biotoxicity of metal elements hinder their widespread application. In this work, inspired by Chinese herbs with anti-inflammatory properties, SB-HHD-CDs were prepared using Scutellaria barbata (SB) and Herba Hedyotis diffusae (HHD) as precursors through a hydrothermal method. The SB-HHD-CDs with excellent thermal and pH stability could rapidly scavenge multiple reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS), such as superoxide anion, hydroxyl radicals, 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl radicals and nitric oxide radicals, at low concentrations. To demonstrate, the as-prepared SB-HHD-CDs were used as cost-effective additives and integrated into a cigarette filter system to effectively remove more than 80% of RONS from cigarette burning. Our work provides a new strategy for the synthesis of novel Se- and metal-free doped CDs-based nanoenzymes with powerful antioxidant capacity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoenzyme
RONS scavenging
Chinese herbs
Cigarette smoke

This work was also supported by the special fund of the Project of the Qilu Young Scholar Program of Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, HR-TEM image, and histogram of the particle size distribution of SB-HHD-CDs. (b)The Raman spectrum and (c) UV–Vis spectra, and (d) 2D fluorescence spectroscopy of SB-HHD-CDs. (e) The PL-decay curves of SB-HHD-CDs. (f) FT-IR spectra of SB-HHD-CDs. (g-i) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively.

(a) Schematic representation of the reduction for DPPH enabled by the SB-HHD-CDs. (b) The kinetic curves of DPPH reduction at different SB-HHD-CDs concentrations. (c) The absorption spectrums of DPPH after 10 min of the reaction with different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs. (d) The SB-HHD-CDs concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of DPPH. (e) the absorption spectra showing the radical eliminating activities of SB-HHD-CDs for •NO. (f) The scavenging efficiency of different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs for •NO.

(a) Schematic representation of the inhibition for •O2 – enabled by the SB-HHD-CDs with NBT as probe. (b) The kinetic curves of •O2 -scavenging at different SB-HHD-CDs concentrations. (c) The absorption spectrums of NBT after 10 min of the reaction with •O2 – at different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs. (d) The SB-HHD-CDs concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of •O2 –. distribution of SB-HHD-CDs. The comparative curves of •O2 -scavenging efficiency at different (e) Temperatures and (f) pH conditions by various concentrations of SOD and SB-HHD-CDs.

(a) Schematic illustration of the oxidation reaction of TMB and the inhibition for •OH enabled by the SB-HHD-CDs. (b) The kinetic curves of •OH scavenging at different SB-HHD-CDs concentrations. (c) The absorption spectra of oxTMB after 5 min of the reaction with •OH at different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs. (d) The SB-HHD-CDs concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of •OH.

(a) The cigarette smoke extract device. (b)The absorption spectra of NBT, the •O2 – indicator, (c) the Fluorescence spectra of TA, the •OH probe, and (d) the fluorescence spectra of Cu-FL, the •NO probe, after exposure to cigarette smoke passing through a cigarette filter without SB-HHD-CDS, or with SB-HHD-CDs incorporated. (e-g) The removal efficiency of as-prepared recycling cigarette holder toward •O2 –, •OH, and •NO.
The comparison of the scavenging ability of different filters for RONS generated during the combustion of cigarettes.
The schematic preparation process procedures for SB-HHD-CDs and the development for cigarette filter application.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as potential antioxidants for the scavenging of multi-reactive oxygen and nitrogen species

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Selenium (Se) and metal-doped carbon dots (CDs) based nanoenzymes have bright prospects as antioxidants; however, the high cost of Se and potential biotoxicity of metal elements hinder their widespread application. In this work, inspired by Chinese herbs with anti-inflammatory properties, SB-HHD-CDs were prepared using Scutellaria barbata (SB) and Herba Hedyotis diffusae (HHD) as precursors through a hydrothermal method. The SB-HHD-CDs with excellent thermal and pH stability could rapidly scavenge multiple reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS), such as superoxide anion, hydroxyl radicals, 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl radicals and nitric oxide radicals, at low concentrations. To demonstrate, the as-prepared SB-HHD-CDs were used as cost-effective additives and integrated into a cigarette filter system to effectively remove more than 80% of RONS from cigarette burning. Our work provides a new strategy for the synthesis of novel Se- and metal-free doped CDs-based nanoenzymes with powerful antioxidant capacity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes with intrinsic enzyme-like properties [1] function as promising supplements or alternatives for natural enzymes and thus have wide applications for antibacterial [2], antioxidant [3], therapeutic [4], and environmental protection [5,6] purposes because of their high stability, feasible modification, and tunable catalytic activity. Among the developed nanozymes, MXenes [7], Pb nanoparticles [8], CeO2 [9], MnO2 [10], and a few specific single-atom catalysts [11,12] exhibited high activity for scavenging reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS). However, the complicated synthetic approaches, high production costs, and unsatisfying RONS trapping capability seriously hamper the practical applications of these nanozymes. Therefore, developing novel nanozymes with efficient RONS scavenging capacity and low cost that have great potential in the future antioxidation field is highly desired.

Carbon dots (CDs) are amorphous carbon particles with characteristic sizes less than 10 nm [13]. Previous investigations have indicated the great potential of CDs-based nanozymes for broad applications owing to their nontoxicity, biological safety, and ecofriendliness [14,15]. Heteroatom doping is an effective strategy to endow CDs with RONS scavenging ability. Xu et al. verified that Se-doped CDs (Se-CDs) possess excellent free radical scavenging properties [16]. Furthermore, Se-CDs were used as broad-spectrum antioxidants for the treatment of acute kidney injury [17]. However, low synthetic yields and the high cost of Se-containing carbon precursors hindered the commercialization of Se-CDs. Notably, some non-Se doped CDs have also been reported to have RONS removal capabilities. For example, Zhang et al. demonstrated the radical scavenging capability of N, S-doped CDs, however, at high concentrations [18]. Xue et al. achieved efficient trapping of RONS by introducing Cu and Zn atoms into CDs, which unfortunately increased the biological toxicity concerns of the material [19]. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop non-Se and nonmetal-doped CDs with potent antioxidant capacity.

Carbon precursors are one of the essential factors affecting the performance of CDs. With low cost and antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiviral, immunomodulatory, and antitumor properties [20,21], Chinese herbs are high-quality precursors for the preparation of CDs. For example, Zheng et al. prepared jujube-derived carbon dots, J-CDs, which could promote the proliferation of red blood cells and lead to the successful treatment of anemia [22]. In another study, Liang et al. prepared Gly-CDs from glycyrrhetinic acid (Gly) with antiviral effects, inhibiting the proliferation of PRRSV by approximately five orders of magnitude [23]. Inspired by these studies, we used Chinese herbs with anti-inflammatory properties as potential candidates for preparing CDs with efficient RONS eradication properties.

The SB-HHD-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. SB (100 mg) and HHD (100 mg) were added to 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After being cooled to room temperature, a large precipitate was removed from the mixture by centrifugation at 10 000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm polyether sulfone membrane to further remove small particles. Then, the filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h in ultrapure water utilizing a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Then, the solution of SB-HHD-CDs was lyophilized. Twenty milligrams of dry SB-HHD-CDs were obtained for further use, with the yield of SB-HHD-CDs being assessed at 10%.

We determined the DPPH scavenging percentage using a calibration curve based on UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm in the presence of various concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs. Different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs (10 μL) were added to DPPH ethanol solution (0.1 mM, 2 mL, pH = 7), followed by vigorous vortexing in the dark. Over a period of 600 s, the UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm was measured every 30 s. It is worth noting that due to the small amount of DPPH and SB-HHD-CDs added, the pH value of the system did not change during the entire experimental process.

In this experiment, •NO was measured by a •NO assay kit. Specifically, 100 μL of sodium nitroprusside (20 mM) and 10 μL of SB-HHD-CDs at various concentrations (from 0 to 25 μg ml−1) were mixed in 2 mL of PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4). After 30 min at 37 °C, 50 μL of Griess reagents R1 and R2 were added for 10 min. Then, the UV--Vis spectrum was obtained.

Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was used as a probe to determine the scavenging efficiency of •O2 –. NBT could react with •O2 –, making the solution turn blue due to the production of formazan, with a peak at 560 nm. Typically, the solution for detection contained riboflavin (6.67 μM), L-methionine (4.33 mM), NBT (25 μM) and different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs (from 0 to 25 μg ml−1) in PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4). Subsequently, an illumination tube of 30 W was used to illuminate the mixture for 300 s. Finally, UV–Visible spectrophotometers were used to determine the absorption of the formazan. The scavenging efficiency of •O2 – was calculated as follows: [(A 1-A 2)/(A 1–A 0)] × 100%, where A 0 is the absorbance of single NBT, A 1, and A 2 and are the absorbance of formazan in the absence and presence of the SB-HHD-CDs, respectively. For pH experiments, sodium hydroxide (10 mM) or hydrochloric acid (10 mM) was used to regulate the pH of the detection solution. At various temperatures, SB-HHD-CDs and natural SOD were incubated for thermal stability research. (20-90℃).

By measuring the absorption peak at 652 nm of oxTMB, we measured the elimination of •OH by SB-HHD-CDs. Briefly, H2O2 (100 mM), TMB (10 mM), and different concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs (from 0 to 100 μg ml−1)were reacted in PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 4.5) for 5 min. Then FeSO4 (10 mM) was added to generate •OH. Subsequently, the absorbance at 652 nm was monitored every 30 s for 300 s. The scavenging efficiency of •OH was calculated as follows: [(A 1-A 2)/(A 1–A 0)] × 100%, where A 0 is the absorbance of TMB alone and A 1, and A 2 are the absorbances of oxTMB in the absence and presence of the SB-HHD-CDs, respectively. Similarly, for pH experiments, sodium hydroxide (0.01 M) or hydrochloric acid (0.01 M) were used to regulate the pH of the detection solution. At various temperatures, SB-HHD-CDs and natural AA were incubated for thermal stability research. (20-90℃).

Experimentally, SB and HHD powder were initially dissolved in ultrapure water, and the obtained solution was then heated in the PTFE lining of an autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown-yellow solution of CDs was filtered and freeze-dried, with a production yield of ≈10%. The structure of the SB-HHD-CDs was studied by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S1, a broad peak at 22.4° is observed, derived from graphitic carbon. According to Fig. 1 a, SB-HHD-CDs exhibit a uniform size of approximately 2.3 nm. Furthermore, the lattice-fringe spacing of SB-HHD-CDs is 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [24]. In addition, the Raman spectrum of SB-HHD-CDs (Fig. 1b) presents a D band at 1203 cm−1 resulting from the sp3-C in the carbon core or originating from surface groups on the CDs. The sp2-C hybridization of CDs is represented by the G band at 1641 cm−1. The ratio of the D peak intensity to the G peak power is 0.74, suggesting a high graphitic-carbon content of SB-HHD-CDs [25]. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 1c, SB-HHD-CDs exhibit an absorption peak at 277 nm for the π-π* transition of carbon cores [26]. Fluorescence emission is an inherent characteristic of CDs. Fig. 1d displays the 2D spectral excitation and emission matrix of SB-HHD-CDs. The spectral emission maximum is spread from the region of 320–360 nm with a narrow excitation of 390–440 nm. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) wavelength redshifted from 402 to 478 nm with increasing wavelength of photoluminescence excitation from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. S2). The different emissive states are responsible for the manifested excitation-dependent emission. The PL decay curve of the SB-HHD-CDs was measured by 340 nm EPLED excitation (Fig. 1e). The results reveal that SB-HHD-CDs contain a fast PL center component (τ: 3.21 ns), associated with the radiative recombination of the carbon core with sp2 hybrid domains, where the electrons are excited through π-π* transitions [27]. This result is in accordance with the UV–Vis spectrum results. Given the excellent optical properties, we also explored the potential application of SB-HHD-CDs in cell imaging. Firstly, the MTT assay was employed to assess the cell toxicity of SB-HHD-CDs, as depicted in Fig. S3. After co-culturing with Hela cells for 24 h, even high concentrations of SB-HHD-CDs (500 μg ml−1) caused negligible harm to Hela cells, indicating the exceptional biocompatibility of SB-HHD-CDs. Subsequently, the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used to evaluate the cell imaging capacity of SB-HHD-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, following incubation with SB-HHD-CDs for 4 h, HeLa cells show blue fluorescence under confocal microscopy suggesting that SB-HHD-CDs offer superior cellular imaging (the detailed process of cell imaging can be found in supporting information).The remarkable biocompatibility and favorable fluorescence emission properties of SB-HHD-CDs suggest its potential as a fluorescent probe for in vitro cell imaging.

The surface chemical properties of SB-HHD-CDs were further revealed using Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1f, the FT-IR spectrum reveals the stretching vibration for –OH/–NH (3261 cm−1). The vibration at 2937 cm−1 is caused by the stretching of C-H [28]. Meanwhile, the absorptions for C = C and C-N can be observed at 1560 and 1412 cm−1, respectively, while the peak at 1091 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of C-O-C [29]. There are four consecutive –CH2 rocking vibrations at 720 cm−1 [30–32]. The SB-HHD-CDs contained C (67.53%), O (26.34%), and N (5.79%), based on the XPS survey data, shown in Fig. S5. Among the C 1 s spectra of SB-HHD-CDs, C-C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-O (286 eV), and C = O/C = N (288.2 eV) can be observed (Fig. 1g) [31], while the O1s spectra contain C = O (531.7 eV) and C-O (533.2 eV) (Fig. 1h) [32]. Furthermore, in Fig. 1i, three components are assigned to graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N [33] as derived from the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum for SB-HHD-CDs. The results are in agreement with the above FT-IR analysis, suggesting that SB-HHD-CDs have rich functional groups on their surfaces.

To examine the RONS scavenging activity of the as-prepared SB-HHDs-CDs, DPPH was selected as the model radical. As an N-centered free radical, DPPH exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 515 nm. When DPPH is in contact with free radical scavengers, the peak decreases due to the formation of the DPPH complex (Fig. 2 a) [34]. The elimination of DPPH represents the anti-RNS ability. As shown in Fig. 2b and 2c, the peak intensity at 517 nm displays a continuous decline as the concentration of SC-HHD-CDs increased from 0 to 25 μg ml−1. Meanwhile, the characteristic purple color of the DPPH solution gradually faded with the increasing amount of SB-HHD-CDs added (Fig. S6). These results indicate the removal of DPPH. The half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of SB-HHD-CDs was calculated as 5 μg ml−1 (Fig. 2d), superior to that of the natural material ascorbic acid (AA). In addition, we explored the ability of SB-HHD-CDs to scavenge •NO, a gaseous RNS, by a nitric oxide assay kit, which could react with •NO to form a diazonium compound accompanied by absorbance at 540 nm. Sodium nitroprusside was used to produce •NO [35]. Fig. 2e shows that SB-HHD-CDs effectively removed •NO in a dose-dependent manner. It is noteworthy that SB-HHD-CDs were able to remove around 75% of •NO at a relatively low concentration (50 μg ml−1) (Fig. 2f). These results demonstrate that SB-HHD-CDs have impressive RNS clearance capability.

To determine the superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity of SB-HHD-CDs, NBT was used as an indicator to assess the scavenging efficiency of SB-HHD-CDs toward •O2 –. NBT is a sensitive superoxide indicator, that can be reduced via •O2 – and form a blue formazan compound with an absorption peak at 560 nm (Fig. 3 a) [36]. As shown in Fig. 3b and 3c, in the absence of SB-HHD-CDs, a continuous increase in absorption at 560 nm is observed with the extension of the reaction time of the reduction of NBT by •O2 –. However, in the presence of SB-HHD-CDs, the absorbance value at 560 nm decreases dramatically and the color of the solution changed gradually from bluish-purple to light yellow (Fig. S7) with increasing concentration of SB-HHD-CDs, attributed to the dismutation of •O2 – by the SB-HHD-CDs. The elimination efficiency of •O2 – was approximately 100% at a concentration of 10 μg ml−1 (Fig. 3d), which is nearly identical to that of natural SOD at 50 U mg−1 (Fig. 3e and Fig. S8). Nevertheless, SB-HHD-CDs retain their activities between 20 and 90 °C, indicating excellent thermal stability, while natural SOD rapidly deactivates at 60 °C (Fig. 3f, Fig. S9a, and S9b). Additionally, the catalytic properties of nanozymes can also be affected by pH conditions. As shown in Fig. S10, over a wide range of pH values, SB-HHD-CDs still maintain high SOD-like activity. Together, these results revealed that SB-HHD-CDs can be used as an alternative to natural SOD.

Hydroxyl radicals are another highly oxidative free radical that can lead to destruction of the cell membrane and DNA, which contributes significantly to oxidative damage [37]. The •OH scavenging ability of SB-HHD-CDs was systematically measured utilizing 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a probe, which can be converted to oxTMB with prominent absorption peaks at 652 nm in the presence of •OH [38]. The classic Fe2+/H2O2 Fenton reaction was utilized to generate •OH in situ in aqueous solution (Fig. 4 a). Based on Fig. 4b and 4c, the absorption peak at 652 nm could be observed in the system without SB-HHD-CDs, indicating the generation of •OH. In the presence of SB-HHD-CDs, the intensity of the absorption peak at 652 nm gradually decreased along with the color of the solution changing from blue to yellow (Fig. S11), suggesting that SB-HHD-CDs are capable of scavenging •OH. When SB-HHD-CDs were increased to concentrations of 100 μg ml−1, they were able to scavenge more than 80.0% •OH (Fig. 4d), which is close to 2 μM of AA (Fig. S12a and S12b). In addition, SB-HHD-CDs maintained a high •OH removal capacity regardless of the temperature, while the activity of AA for removing •OH decreased rapidly when it reached a temperature of 60 °C (Fig. S13a and S13b). In addition, SB-HHD-CDs can still maintain an effective capacity for •OH scavenging in various pH environments (Fig. S14). The above results indicate that SB-HHD-CDs exhibit excellent ability to remove RONS.

In light of the above findings, for demonstration, we filled cigarette filters with SB-HHD-CDs (10 mg) to remove the RONS generated during tobacco combustion. To evaluate the scavenging efficacy of RONS, smoke generated from cigarettes incorporated with SB-HHD-CDs was exposed to a variety of solutions that contained the indicator (Fig. 5 a). NBT was used to measure the amount of •O2 – produced by smoking cigarettes. According to Fig. 5b, NBT reacted with the generated •O2 – in the cigarette without SB-HHD-CDs addition, causing a clear increase in the absorption peak at 560 nm. In contrast, the absorptions are significantly lower in cigarettes with SB-HHD-CDs, indicating excellent •O2 – scavenging properties. Meanwhile, the efficiencies of •O2 – scavenging are approximately 84.81% in cigarettes incorporating SB-HHD-CDs. Moreover, terephthalic acid (TA) is used as a fluorescent probe to detect •OH. As a typical fluorescent probe, TA can be oxidized by •OH to form a fluorescent compound with an emission wavelength at 440 nm [39]. It is visible in Fig. 5c that a clear fluorescence signal is observed without SB-HHD-CDs addition. Decreasing the fluorescence intensity reveals the trapping efficiency of •OH to be 77.45%. Furthermore, Cu-FL, a copper-fluorescein conjugate, was used to measure •NO [40]. A peak in fluorescence emission at 535 nm was observed (Fig. 5d) when Cu-FL reacted with the •NO generated by the cigarette without SB-HHD-CDs. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of the cigarette with SB-HHD-CDs was significantly reduced, indicating 68.46% efficiency at scavenging •NO.

In summary, SB and HHD, Chinese herbs with anti-inflammatory potency, were utilized as precursors to construct Se- and metal-free doped carbon dots (SB-HHD-CDs). We determined that the as-prepared SB-HHD-CDs exhibit superior scavenging abilities against multiple RONS, including •O2 –, •OH, DPPH, and •NO. Despite high temperatures or harsh pH conditions, SB-HHD-CDs were still efficiently capable of scavenging RONS. The multiple antioxidant capacities, high thermal stability, and low cost make SB-HHD-CDs a potential cigarette filter additive to remove more than 80% of RONS produced from cigarette combustion. This work suggested a cost-effective approach for the fabrication of CDs-based nanoenzymes with effective RONS scavenging capacity for specific functions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 5210128, 52225406 and U1806219). This work was also supported by the special fund of the Project of the Qilu Young Scholar Program of Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142338.
